mappings.rationale: Directly covers the history of Africa, including West African kingdoms, empires, the slave trade, and European colonial encounters discussed in this section.
West African religious practices, including the Islamic revolutions and jihads mentioned in the section, are grounded in the broader context of African religions.
The section covers both the indigenous empires of West Africa (Mali, Songhai, Ghana) and the European colonial enterprises that supplanted them.
The Islamic revolutions in the western Sudan, the Fulani and Hausa jihads, and the role of Islam in shaping West African societies are central topics of this section.
The section traces the precolonial and early colonial period in West Africa, providing the historical substrate that postcolonial theory interrogates.
The trans-Saharan trade routes and Timbuktu as a cultural and commercial centre connect to broader networks of premodern long-distance trade.
The section explicitly notes the importance of archaeological sources and historiographic problems for understanding West African civilizations.
The ethnography of West Africa and the character and achievements of its civilizations involve anthropological methods and frameworks.
The Atlantic slave trade from c. 1600-1860 connected West Africa to the Americas; the development of European slave trade routes and the African slave merchant class are major topics of this section.
European exploration of the Guinea coast and the West African interior is a key aspect of the precolonial period covered in this section.
British colonial settlements in Sierra Leone, the Gold Coast, Lagos, and Ashanti from c. 1800 represent early British imperial expansion into West Africa.
The European antislavery movements and the Guinea coastlands' role in the abolition struggle are explicitly discussed in the section.
The spread of Islam through the western Sudan, the Fulani jihads, and the spiritual leadership of figures like Usman dan Fodio represent Islam's global reach into West Africa.
The political structures of West African monarchies and empires, and the collapse of indigenous states under European pressure, are foundational to understanding African politics.
